content.text: Standard web service demand forecasting predicts request volume as a function of time, user growth, and business seasonality. GenAI traffic forecasting must forecast four additional dimensions that standard approaches ignore: token volume per request (which determines compute cost and duration), multi-turn session depth (which multiplies effective compute by the number of agent turns), model tier distribution (which can shift per-request cost by an order of magnitude), and the interaction between these dimensions under product feature changes.

Demand forecasting for GenAI traffic is the practice of projecting future compute resource consumption — measured in token-weighted GPU compute units rather than raw request count — to enable capacity planning, budget forecasting, and autoscaling policy configuration. Unlike standard web service forecasting, a GenAI traffic forecast must model the distribution of request characteristics, not just aggregate volume.

A single 1,000-token completion request consumes approximately 50x more compute than a 20-token classification request. If the proportion of long-completion requests increases due to a product change — for example, enabling multi-document summarisation — the aggregate GPU consumption grows far faster than request count. A forecast that does not account for this distribution shift will underestimate capacity requirements despite predicting the correct number of requests.

The forecast is consumed by three downstream processes: capacity planning (how many GPU instances to reserve or provision for the next 30-90 days), autoscaling policy configuration (what scaling thresholds and rates to set for the next planning cycle), and budget forecasting (what the cloud compute bill will be for the next quarter). Each consumer requires a different forecast horizon and precision level.

GenAI infrastructure costs are non-linear with traffic growth. A 2x increase in users can produce a 6x increase in GPU compute costs if the new users happen to be heavier users of multi-turn features. Without demand forecasting that accounts for token volume and session depth, finance and engineering leadership make capacity and budget decisions with an incomplete picture of the cost trajectory.

Capacity shortfalls in GenAI systems manifest as latency spikes and rate limit errors rather than server crashes, because the LLM provider throttles requests before the system completely fails. Users experience this as slow responses and intermittent failures. If the capacity shortfall was predictable — and token-weighted forecasting can predict it 30-60 days in advance — the service degradation is an avoidable operational failure.

Conversely, over-provisioning GPU compute for an agentic AI system is expensive. A GPU A100 instance costs approximately $3-5/hour on-demand. A system over-provisioned by 3x to accommodate a traffic peak that was actually a one-time event wastes tens of thousands of dollars per month. Accurate demand forecasting with appropriate confidence intervals enables precise provisioning: reserve capacity at the P95 forecast rather than 2x the peak.
Token-weighted forecasts produce GPU cost projections that finance can use for quarterly budget planning, replacing reactive cost surprises with planned allocations.
P95 confidence interval forecasts allow capacity to be provisioned at the headroom level appropriate for the SLO, not at an arbitrary multiple of peak.

Forecasting session depth and token volume distributions enables autoscaling policies calibrated to the actual compute-per-request, reducing both under-scaling (latency spikes) and over-scaling (cost waste).
Forecast models that include feature flag states can estimate the traffic impact of enabling a new feature before it rolls out, enabling proactive capacity provisioning.
Accurate 90-day token volume forecasts provide the committed-use baseline for negotiating reserved capacity or volume pricing with LLM API providers.
GenAI traffic patterns change rapidly as product features evolve. A forecast model trained on data from before a major feature launch may have poor accuracy for post-launch traffic.
Forecasting token volume distributions rather than just request count requires richer feature engineering and more data than standard time-series methods.
When the mix of request types shifts (e.g., users shifting to longer prompts), the historical patterns on which the model was trained become less representative and forecast accuracy degrades.
A newly launched feature has no traffic history. Forecasting its traffic requires business estimates from product management, which introduce uncertainty that cannot be reduced by more data.
The forecasting pipeline has three stages: feature engineering from raw traffic logs, model training and ensemble blending, and forecast output generation with confidence intervals.

Feature engineering transforms raw request logs into model inputs. The most important GenAI-specific features are: total tokens per time window (input plus output), compute units (a weighted token count that approximates GPU time), effective request count (requests multiplied by tool-call depth for agentic workloads), and model tier distribution (fraction of requests on each model size). Temporal features (hour of day, day of week, business-hours indicator) capture the cyclical patterns that drive most short-term demand variation.

The model ensemble combines a seasonal time-series model (Prophet or SARIMA) with a feature-rich gradient-boosted regressor (XGBoost or LightGBM). The seasonal model captures calendar patterns and long-run trends; the regressor captures the relationship between traffic characteristics (token volume, session depth) and total compute consumption. The ensemble blends both with weights calibrated by walk-forward backtesting.

The output is a 30-day hourly forecast with P50, P95, and P99 confidence intervals on compute units. The P50 is used for expected-cost budgeting; the P95 is used for capacity reservation; the P99 is used for autoscaling ceiling configuration.

attrs.alt: GenAI Traffic Demand Forecasting Pipeline: data sources through feature engineering, model ensemble, and capacity/autoscale/budget outputs

content.text: Data flows from historical traffic metrics, product roadmap events, and business calendar into feature engineering. Three models (Prophet, XGBoost, exponential smoothing) run in parallel and are blended with backtesting-calibrated weights. Outputs drive three consumers: capacity planning (GPU reservation), autoscale policy (scaling thresholds), and budget alerts (cost forecast).

Token-weighted demand forecasting is appropriate when the system handles mixed-length requests, when session depth varies (agentic systems with tool calls), or when feature changes can shift the token distribution. It is most valuable at scale: above a few hundred concurrent users, the difference between request-count forecasting and token-weighted forecasting becomes large enough to matter for capacity and cost decisions.

For small deployments (under 100 requests/day), simple heuristic-based provisioning (deploy at 3x observed peak, scale manually as traffic grows) is more cost-effective than building a forecasting pipeline. The investment in the pipeline is justified when the cost of over-provisioning or under-provisioning exceeds the cost of maintaining the pipeline.
Step 1: Build the GenAI feature matrix

# forecast/features.py
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def build_genai_features(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Build feature matrix from raw GenAI request logs.

    Input df columns:
      timestamp, request_count, input_tokens, output_tokens,
      tool_call_count, session_turns, model_tier
    """
    df = df.copy()
    df["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp"])
    df = df.sort_values("timestamp").set_index("timestamp")

    # Volume features
    df["total_tokens"] = df["input_tokens"] + df["output_tokens"]
    df["tokens_per_request"] = df["total_tokens"] / df["request_count"].clip(lower=1)

    # Compute-intensity features
    # Proxy for GPU time: output tokens dominate inference compute
    df["compute_units"] = df["output_tokens"] * 0.0015 + df["input_tokens"] * 0.0003

    # Multi-turn session depth (tool-call amplification)
    df["effective_requests"] = (
        df["request_count"] * (1 + df["tool_call_count"] / df["request_count"].clip(lower=1))
    )

    # Temporal features
    df["hour"] = df.index.hour
    df["day_of_week"] = df.index.dayofweek
    df["is_weekend"] = (df["day_of_week"] >= 5).astype(int)
    df["is_business_hours"] = ((df["hour"] >= 9) & (df["hour"] < 18) & ~df["is_weekend"].astype(bool)).astype(int)

    # Rolling statistics (lag features for ML model)
    for lag in [1, 7, 28]:
        df[f"compute_lag_{lag}d"] = df["compute_units"].shift(lag)
        df[f"request_lag_{lag}d"] = df["request_count"].shift(lag)

    # 7-day rolling mean and std (trend features)
    df["compute_rolling_mean_7d"] = df["compute_units"].rolling("7D").mean()
    df["compute_rolling_std_7d"]  = df["compute_units"].rolling("7D").std()

    return df.dropna()
Step 2: Train and evaluate the ensemble model

# forecast/ensemble.py
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from prophet import Prophet
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_percentage_error

class GenAIForecastEnsemble:
    def __init__(self, horizon_days: int = 30):
        self.horizon = horizon_days
        self.prophet = Prophet(
            seasonality_mode="multiplicative",
            weekly_seasonality=True,
            daily_seasonality=True,
            changepoint_prior_scale=0.05,
        )
        self.xgb = XGBRegressor(
            n_estimators=300,
            max_depth=6,
            learning_rate=0.05,
            subsample=0.8,
        )
        self.scaler = StandardScaler()
        self._weights = {"prophet": 0.4, "xgb": 0.6}

    def fit(self, df: pd.DataFrame, feature_cols: list[str]):
        # Prophet expects columns: ds (datetime), y (target)
        prophet_df = df[["compute_units"]].reset_index()
        prophet_df.columns = ["ds", "y"]
        self.prophet.fit(prophet_df)

        # XGBoost on engineered features
        X = df[feature_cols].values
        y = df["compute_units"].values
        X_scaled = self.scaler.fit_transform(X)
        self.xgb.fit(X_scaled, y)
        self._feature_cols = feature_cols

    def predict(self, future_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        # Prophet forecast
        future_prophet = self.prophet.make_future_dataframe(periods=self.horizon * 24, freq="H")
        prophet_fc = self.prophet.predict(future_prophet)[["ds", "yhat", "yhat_lower", "yhat_upper"]]
        prophet_fc = prophet_fc.tail(self.horizon * 24)

        # XGBoost forecast (rolling simulation for future)
        X_fut = future_df[self._feature_cols].values
        X_fut_scaled = self.scaler.transform(X_fut)
        xgb_fc = self.xgb.predict(X_fut_scaled)

        # Blend
        w = self._weights
        blended = w["prophet"] * prophet_fc["yhat"].values + w["xgb"] * xgb_fc

        return pd.DataFrame({
            "timestamp": prophet_fc["ds"].values,
            "forecast_compute_units": blended,
            "lower_bound": prophet_fc["yhat_lower"].values,
            "upper_bound": prophet_fc["yhat_upper"].values,
        })

    def score(self, df: pd.DataFrame, feature_cols: list[str]) -> float:
        preds = self.predict(df)
        return mean_absolute_percentage_error(df["compute_units"].values, preds["forecast_compute_units"].values)
Step 3: Convert forecasts to capacity and cost plans

# forecast/capacity_plan.py
import pandas as pd

GPU_COMPUTE_PER_UNIT = 0.12   # fractional GPU-seconds per compute unit
GPU_UTILISATION_TARGET = 0.65  # target 65% utilisation to leave headroom
COST_PER_GPU_HOUR = 3.50       # $/GPU-hour (example: A100 on-demand)

def compute_units_to_gpu_hours(forecast_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = forecast_df.copy()
    # Convert compute units to GPU-hours needed at target utilisation
    df["raw_gpu_hours"] = df["forecast_compute_units"] * GPU_COMPUTE_PER_UNIT
    df["required_gpu_hours"] = df["raw_gpu_hours"] / GPU_UTILISATION_TARGET

    # P95 headroom: use upper_bound for reservation planning
    df["p95_gpu_hours"] = df["upper_bound"] * GPU_COMPUTE_PER_UNIT / GPU_UTILISATION_TARGET

    # Cost projections
    df["forecast_cost_usd"] = df["required_gpu_hours"] * COST_PER_GPU_HOUR
    df["p95_cost_usd"]      = df["p95_gpu_hours"] * COST_PER_GPU_HOUR

    return df[["timestamp", "required_gpu_hours", "p95_gpu_hours",
               "forecast_cost_usd", "p95_cost_usd"]]

def generate_capacity_report(capacity_df: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    weekly = capacity_df.set_index("timestamp").resample("W").sum()
    return {
        "total_forecast_gpu_hours_30d": capacity_df["required_gpu_hours"].sum(),
        "p95_gpu_hours_30d": capacity_df["p95_gpu_hours"].sum(),
        "total_forecast_cost_30d_usd": capacity_df["forecast_cost_usd"].sum(),
        "peak_daily_gpu_hours": capacity_df.set_index("timestamp")["required_gpu_hours"].resample("D").sum().max(),
        "weekly_breakdown": weekly.to_dict(),
    }
Step 4: Walk-forward backtest to validate accuracy

# tests/forecast/test_backtest.py
import pytest, pandas as pd, numpy as np
from forecast.features import build_genai_features
from forecast.ensemble import GenAIForecastEnsemble

def test_forecast_mape_within_threshold(historical_df):
    """Walk-forward backtest: MAPE must be < 15% on held-out last 30 days."""
    features = build_genai_features(historical_df)
    feature_cols = [c for c in features.columns if c != "compute_units"]
    cutoff = features.index.max() - pd.Timedelta(days=30)
    train = features[features.index <= cutoff]
    test = features[features.index > cutoff]
    model = GenAIForecastEnsemble(horizon_days=30)
    model.fit(train, feature_cols)
    mape = model.score(test, feature_cols)
    assert mape < 0.15, f"Forecast MAPE {mape:.1%} exceeds 15% threshold"

@pytest.fixture
def historical_df():
    # 90 days of synthetic hourly data
    np.random.seed(42)
    n = 90 * 24
    ts = pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=n, freq="H")
    base = 1000 + 200 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * pd.Series(ts).dt.hour / 24)
    df = pd.DataFrame({
        "timestamp": ts,
        "request_count": (base + np.random.normal(0, 50, n)).clip(0).astype(int),
        "input_tokens": (base * 500 + np.random.normal(0, 5000, n)).clip(0),
        "output_tokens": (base * 200 + np.random.normal(0, 2000, n)).clip(0),
        "tool_call_count": np.random.poisson(2, n),
        "session_turns": np.random.poisson(3, n),
        "model_tier": "standard",
    })
    return df

Minimal forecast for a single-model deployment serving uniform request sizes: a 30-day moving average of daily request counts with a 20% headroom multiplier. This is appropriate when request characteristics are homogeneous, there are no multi-turn sessions, and traffic patterns are stable.

Full ensemble forecast for a multi-turn agentic AI platform with three model tiers: the feature matrix includes token volume per tier, tool-call depth per session, and product feature flag states. The XGBoost model is retrained weekly on a 90-day rolling window. Walk-forward backtesting on the last 30 days must achieve MAPE under 15% before the model is deployed. Outputs are published to a Grafana dashboard and consumed by the autoscaling policy via a weekly Airflow DAG.

A SaaS company operating an AI writing assistant launched a 'long-form document mode' feature in Q3 that enabled users to generate 5,000-word documents. The marketing team projected a 20% increase in active users from the launch. Engineering provisioned 20% more GPU capacity.

Two weeks after launch, infrastructure costs increased by 180% while user count increased by 22%. The root cause: long-form document mode requests consumed 8-12x more output tokens than the baseline average request, and the new feature attracted exactly the high-volume users who were most likely to use it intensively. The request-count forecast was accurate; the compute forecast failed because it did not model the token distribution shift introduced by the new feature.

The post-incident response: the team built a token-weighted forecast model that incorporated feature flag states as features. Before the next feature launch (multi-document comparison), they ran a scenario analysis by setting the feature flag feature in the model to 1.0 (full rollout) and re-running the forecast. The projected 4x compute increase for a 30% user increase informed a phased rollout plan with capacity pre-provisioned at each rollout stage.
Request count is a poor proxy for compute consumption when request characteristics vary. Always forecast compute units (token-weighted) in addition to request count.
Ignoring session depth for agentic workloads:
A multi-turn agent session generates N requests per user interaction where N is the turn count. Forecasting without session depth significantly underestimates traffic for agentic workloads.
A point estimate without confidence intervals cannot drive capacity planning. P95 and P99 bounds are required to set capacity headroom.
A model trained before a major feature launch is trained on a different distribution than the one it will forecast. Retrain or update within one week of a major feature change.
Shifting users from a smaller to a larger model (e.g., adding access to an extended-context model) can 4-10x the compute cost per user without changing request count.
Use compute units, not request count, as the primary forecast target:
Compute units capture the actual GPU consumption that drives infrastructure cost and capacity.
Maintain separate sub-forecasts by model tier and request type:
When the mix of tiers or types is likely to shift, forecasting sub-populations and summing produces more accurate totals than forecasting the aggregate directly.
Measure MAPE on the last 30 days every week. A MAPE increase of more than 5 percentage points over two weeks signals distribution shift and triggers model retraining.
Include product roadmap events as model features:
Planned feature launches, user onboarding campaigns, and pricing changes affect traffic. Encode them as binary features in the forecast model.
Publish forecasts to a shared dashboard:
Make the 30-day forecast visible to finance, engineering, and product management. This creates shared accountability for the accuracy of planning assumptions.

The forecasting pipeline runs daily (or weekly for long-horizon plans) as a batch job, not in the request path. Performance requirements are a wall-clock training time under 30 minutes and forecast generation under 5 minutes for a 30-day hourly forecast. These are achievable with XGBoost on a standard compute instance.

As the traffic volume grows, the feature matrix grows in size but the forecasting problem does not change in structure. The main scalability consideration is historical data volume: training on 90 days of hourly data with 30 features is manageable on a single machine; extending to 2 years of minute-level data may require distributed training or a reduced feature set for the lookback period.

Model accuracy typically improves with more historical data up to 6-12 months, after which additional history adds little value because traffic patterns change enough over longer periods to make older history less relevant than recent data. A rolling 90-day training window with weekly retraining balances recency against stability.
Strong on calendar seasonality; handles holidays
Daily retraining and forecast generation DAGs
Publishes P50/P95/P99 forecast to shared view
Similar to ensemble; more data needed

The forecasting pipeline must be reliable because capacity planning and budget decisions depend on it. Failures in the pipeline that go undetected — a data pipeline bug that skews the training data, a model rollback that silently lowers accuracy — lead to incorrect capacity decisions that take weeks to surface.

Validate input data before each training run: check for gaps in the time series, outliers beyond 3 standard deviations, and unexpected drops to zero that indicate logging failures rather than real traffic drops.
Alert when the 7-day rolling MAPE exceeds 20%. This signals that the model is no longer accurate for the current traffic pattern and needs retraining or feature engineering changes.

Before publishing a forecast, validate that week-over-week forecast changes are within plausible bounds (e.g., flag if the 30-day forecast exceeds 5x the current traffic without a known feature launch).
Forecasts contain information about traffic patterns and product launch plans. Restrict access to the forecast dashboard to engineering and finance teams.

Think of GenAI demand forecasting as predicting water flow in a river system rather than rain volume. The rain (user requests) is only part of what determines the downstream flow (GPU compute). The width of each tributary (token volume per request), the branching factor of the river network (tool-call depth), and whether the river is running through a mountain pass (model tier) all determine how much water moves through the system. Counting the rain without modelling the river system will produce a flow estimate that is consistently wrong when the geography changes.

The most important conceptual shift from standard web forecasting is that the unit of capacity planning is compute-minutes, not requests. When everything is measured in compute-minutes, the effect of a new feature that doubles output token length is immediately visible as a doubling of the per-request compute budget, and the capacity plan updates automatically.
Forecast compute units (token-weighted) rather than request count; for agentic systems, also forecast effective request count (requests × tool-call depth) to capture session amplification.
Use an ensemble of a seasonal model (Prophet) and a feature-rich regressor (XGBoost) to capture both calendar patterns and the impact of request-characteristic features.
Publish P50, P95, and P99 confidence interval forecasts; use P50 for cost budgeting, P95 for capacity reservation, and P99 for autoscaling ceiling configuration.
Retrain models within one week of major feature launches; validate with a walk-forward backtest requiring MAPE under 15%.
Include product roadmap feature flag states as model features to enable pre-launch capacity scenario analysis.
Monitor forecast accuracy weekly with a rolling MAPE metric; alert and trigger retraining when MAPE exceeds 20%.
